text: Hi, I'm Arati from AWS.
Hi, I'm Carlos Carlos from Opportune, and this is my architecture.
We've got an architecture here, but I would love to understand what business challenge
are you trying to solve with AWS solutions?
So the business challenge that we were trying to solve at Opportune was knowing where our
sensitive data was. It was vastly important for us to know, especially sensitive data for our members
information to protect it. That was the number one challenge that we wanted to solve.
Help me understand what kind of data is it and why is it important to Opportune?
We provide financial services to folks so that typically are left out of the financial mainstream.
Our members data is very important to us. So things like social security numbers,
ITINs, government IDs, right? All this information is what we need to provide services to our members.
And it is of utmost importance to us to protect that data.
Awesome. So why don't you show us how you utilize Macy to achieve this?
Sure. Yeah, so Macy is a data discovery tool. And the main thing about Macy is, you know, you can
connect multiple, most organizations have more than one AWS account, right? We started from our
perspective, our priority was production data. So we looked at our, you know, we connected our
production accounts. And for every account that you connect Macy to, you have an S3 bucket, right,
that you have to set up for that account. Once we connected Macy to our accounts, it quickly did an
inventory of all our S3 buckets. And it was just a matter of, you know, us determining which buckets
we wanted to scan out of the box. You know, the end result really is to improve our data,
data security posture management, right, our DSPM. Improve and protect our customer data is the end
goal. So when we run these scans on the S3 buckets, right, and we're running our scans, whether they're
manual or scheduled or whatever cadence we have, we have findings that are coming in here. Again,
as I mentioned, no PII, no sensitive information is in the buckets, you know.
So basically just metadata.
It's metadata. And these are S3 buckets that we own. They're in our account, right? That's important as well.
Because ultimately, the results come in here, you know, again, it's the number of SSNs in this
bucket or number of items or data, whatever it is that you're looking for, depending on the
sensitivity of the data. So you have the data that goes into the buckets. And we have other tools
that, you know, services with AWS, such as Amazon Athena here, right, that looks into and queries
these buckets. Amazon Athena is a SQL-based discovery, you know, query tool that
basically looks at the information from the findings. And to your point about what are we
doing with this, ultimately, the end result is identifying the findings there, finding the owners
of the buckets, and then ultimately remediating, right? How do we remediate? We identify the owners,
additional services that we have, you know, in our use case, we drop the data as well into some
MySQL data lake that we've built. And you don't want to give users a big CSV file or Excel file with,
here, here's all your findings, right? Here's, you want to tell a better story. We do that by
peering into, you know, the information directly from the S3 bucket via Athena and putting that
information in a dashboard, using QuickSight, other tools like Tableau, Power BI, you know, whatever
analytic tool that you have, any business intelligent tool to tell a better story of saying, hey, here
are our top 10, top 20, top 30, you know, risky buckets by type of PII. And here's the owners that
we have, we want to remediate with. And that's how we go and talk to the owners, remediate, we show them,
and we work together to remediate, delete the data, archive it, remove the bucket if it's no longer in
use, right? Glue is also part of that equation. But primarily, it's the process of Amazon sending
the findings to Athena, you know, and via some automations with Glue, we have, you know, we have
the additional processes that get the data, load the data, aggregate it, and then we ultimately report it
and talk to our data owners. But on an average, what would you say the accuracy is, like for something
landing into a bucket, being detected by Macy and then going through your like visualization workflow here?
Yeah. So, you know, for patterns and regexes, like SSNs and ITINs, some of those top really critical
sensitive types, our accuracy, you know, we found with Macy, automated sensitive discovery was 95%.
Got it. Thank you, Carlos. Appreciate sharing the architecture with us.
You're welcome. Thank you for having me.
